title: Norovirus
body_html:
Background
  • Noroviruses, also called Norwalk-like viruses, are a group of RNA viruses with over 30 genotypes (GI, GII, and GIV infect humans).
  • They are the leading cause of viral gastroenteritis in adults in the UK.
  • In young children (<5), rotavirus has traditionally been the most common, although norovirus is increasingly important since rotavirus vaccination.

Transmission
  • Spread is typically person-to-person via the faeco-oral route.
  • Transmission also occurs via aerosolised particles from vomit/diarrhoea, contaminated food and water, and contaminated surfaces.
  • Very low infectious dose (10–100 viral particles) makes norovirus highly contagious.

Pathophysiology
  • Noroviruses infect the small bowel epithelium (enterocytes), causing villous blunting, malabsorption, and secretory diarrhoea.
  • Immunity is short-lived and strain-specific, so reinfections are common.
  • Some individuals are genetically resistant to certain strains due to their histo-blood group antigen (“secretor status”).

Clinical features
  • Incubation period: typically 24–48 hours (median ~24 hours).
  • Symptoms: watery diarrhoea, nausea, malaise, fever, and often profuse vomiting.
  • Illness usually lasts 12–60 hours, though longer in children, the elderly, or immunocompromised patients.

Diagnosis
  • Usually a clinical diagnosis.
  • PCR is the most sensitive and specific test. ELISA antigen detection is less sensitive.
  • Laboratory confirmation is generally reserved for outbreak/public health purposes.
  • For individual management, identifying the causative organism has little clinical impact.

Management
  • Supportive care with oral or IV rehydration.
  • Hygiene and infection control measures are key.
  • Antiviral therapy is not indicated.

Epidemiology
  • Outbreaks are common in hospitals, care homes, and schools.
  • More frequent in winter but can occur year-round.
  • Norovirus is not a notifiable disease in the UK, but institutional outbreaks are usually reported to Public health teams.

Key Points
  • Noroviruses, also called Norwalk like viruses, are a group of viruses with over 30 genotypes, and are the main cause of viral gastroenteritis in adults in the UK
  • In young children (<5) rotavirus is the most common
  • Transmission can be from person to person spread (feco-oral or aerosol particles from vomit/diarrhoea) or from contaminated surfaces or contaminated food and water
  • Diagnosis by PCR/ELISA may be useful for public health reasons.
  • The individual management of viral gastroenteritis is based on rehydration and sanitation measures
  • Small bowel epithelium acts as viral host
  • Watery diarrhoea along with fever, malaise and nausea +/- vomiting are typical features.
  • Incubation period is typically 24 to 48 hours
  • Symptoms typically last 12 to 60 hours
